coredata.dc:title: Paper microfluidic device using carbon dots to detect glucose and lactate in saliva samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Rossini, Eduardo Luiz
Milani, Maria Izabel
Lima, Liliane Spazzapam
Pezza, Helena Redigolo
coredata.dc:description: Bioanalyses are commonly performed with blood or serum samples. However, these analyses often require invasive and painful blood collection using a needle or finger pricking. Saliva is an alternative and very attractive biological medium for performing clinical analyses, since it contains many types of clinically relevant biomarkers and compounds. Its collection is straightforward and can be achieved in a non-invasive and stress-free way. However, the analytes are frequently present at low concentrations, while the viscosity of whole saliva hinders its analysis using paper devices, especially those with multiple layers (3D-μPADs). This work explores the use of a simple, fast, and low-cost saliva sample pretreatment using a cotton-paper-syringe filtration system, allowing the analysis of saliva samples using multilayer paper devices. The proposed methodology employs the oxidation of glucose and lactate, catalyzed by specific oxidase enzymes, producing hydrogen peroxide. The detection is based on the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots in the presence of hydrogen peroxidase. The concentrations of the analytes showed good linear correlations with the fluorescence quenching, with LODs of 2.60 × 10−6 and 8.14 × 10−7 mol L−1 for glucose and lactate, respectively. The proposed method presented satisfactory intra-day and inter-day repeatabilities, with %RSD values in the range 3.82–6.61%. The enzymatic systems proved to be specific for the analytes and the matrix had no significant influence on the glucose and lactate determinations. The proposed methodology was successfully applied to saliva and serum samples and was validated using certified material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Paper platform
Filtration
Glucose
Lactate
Saliva
Serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dot synthesis and reagent preparation
Analysis of biological samples using the 3D-μPAD
Determination of the figures of merit

Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support provided by the São Paulo State Research Foundation (FAPESP, grants #2016/20847-6 and #2015/21733-1) and the Brazilian National Research Council (CNPq, grant #140503/2016-1), and to Flávia Pavarina (NAC-UNESP) for providing the certified samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the saliva sample preparation procedure. (a) Cotton ball introduced in the patient’s mouth; (b) cotton ball with saliva transferred to a syringe containing four circles of filter paper; (c) introduction of the piston and collection of the filtered sample in a vial.

Step-by-step illustration of the fabrication and use of the 3D-μPAD. (a) 3D-μPAD design; (b) wax printing; (c) melting the wax to form the hydrophobic barriers; (d) 3D-μPAD after cutting and gluing the sample holder; (e) addition of the fluorescence reagent dispersion and enzyme mixtures in the last layer; (f) folding and insertion of the 3D-μPAD between the metal apparatus and two magnetic stainless steel sheets; (g) sample insertion on the sample holder; (h) cutting of the last layer and insertion in a UV chamber for acquisition of a digital image; (i) digital image analysis.

Schematic illustration of the proposed mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the CDs when reacting with glucose in the presence of GOx and HRP, or with lactate in the presence of LOx and HRP. The inserts show the fluorescence of the CDs before and after the reaction.
Linear ranges, regression equations, coefficients of variation, and LOD values obtained for analysis of glucose and lactate using the proposed method.
Determinations of glucose and lactate in the certified serum samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: bThe critical value at the 95% confidence level, with two degrees of freedom, was 4.303.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of glucose and lactate in the saliva samples using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Paper microfluidic device using carbon dots to detect glucose and lactate in saliva samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto de Química, Universidade Estadual Paulista “Júlio de Mesquita Filho” – UNESP, Rua Prof. Francisco Degni 55, C.P. 355, 14800-900 Araraquara, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidade Estadual Paulista “Júlio de Mesquita Filho” – UNESP

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instituto de Química, Universidade Estadual Paulista “Júlio de Mesquita Filho” – UNESP, Rua Prof. Francisco Degni 55, C.P. 355, 14800-900 Araraquara, SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bioanalyses are commonly performed with blood or serum samples. However, these analyses often require invasive and painful blood collection using a needle or finger pricking. Saliva is an alternative and very attractive biological medium for performing clinical analyses, since it contains many types of clinically relevant biomarkers and compounds. Its collection is straightforward and can be achieved in a non-invasive and stress-free way. However, the analytes are frequently present at low concentrations, while the viscosity of whole saliva hinders its analysis using paper devices, especially those with multiple layers (3D-μPADs). This work explores the use of a simple, fast, and low-cost saliva sample pretreatment using a cotton-paper-syringe filtration system, allowing the analysis of saliva samples using multilayer paper devices. The proposed methodology employs the oxidation of glucose and lactate, catalyzed by specific oxidase enzymes, producing hydrogen peroxide. The detection is based on the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots in the presence of hydrogen peroxidase. The concentrations of the analytes showed good linear correlations with the fluorescence quenching, with LODs of 2.60 × 10−6 and 8.14 × 10−7 mol L−1 for glucose and lactate, respectively. The proposed method presented satisfactory intra-day and inter-day repeatabilities, with %RSD values in the range 3.82–6.61%. The enzymatic systems proved to be specific for the analytes and the matrix had no significant influence on the glucose and lactate determinations. The proposed methodology was successfully applied to saliva and serum samples and was validated using certified material.

ce:section.ce:para: Since paper was first used as a microfluidic platform in 2007 [1], the microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD) has become a powerful tool for applications in different fields including environmental analysis [2], food analysis [3], and clinical diagnostics [4]. Essentially, these paper-based devices are paper platforms containing hydrophilic channels that are delimited by hydrophobic barriers constructed using wax-printing, photolithography, stamping, or dipping, among other methods [5]. The μPAD combines the advantages of a paper platform, such as low cost, easy availability, flexibility, hydrophilicity, light weight, biocompatibility and power-free fluid transport, with microfluidic control of fluid transport, small sample volume, and low reagent usage [6–8].

Colorimetry and electrochemical methods are the most commonly used detection techniques for analysis using paper devices, because they allow the use of simple, easy-to-use, low cost, and portable instrumentation. The utilization of fluorescence as a detection method for paper devices is still limited, compared to other techniques [9,10]. It offers advantages similar to those of colorimetry, providing analytical results by direct visual read-out. Furthermore, it can improve the sensitivity and selectivity of clinical analyses, due to its low detection limits [11].

Fluorescence is an attractive detection method for the development of μPAD techniques. However, the lack of available fluorescence reactions still limits its application. One option is to exploit the use of carbon dots (CDs), which are carbon nanoparticles that have attracted considerable attention due to their unique properties. Compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QD), CDs present advantages such as low toxicity, good water solubility, high biocompatibility, simple and inexpensive synthesis routes, abundant sources of precursors, and rich contents of functional groups, facilitating surface modification or functionalization. At the same time, they possess QD properties including high photostability, resistance to photobleaching, and good photoluminescence [12,13]. Consequently, they have been employed in bioimaging [14], biosensing [15], catalysis [16], drug delivery [17], and sensors for organic compounds [18–20] and metals [21,22]. The introduction of CDs increases the potential of new fluorescence methods using paper platforms, especially for clinical applications, because this association can combine the low detection limits of fluorescence methods with the simplicity and low cost of paper devices, enabling the creation of ASSURED (Affordable, Sensitive, Specific, User-friendly, Robust and rapid, Equipment-free, and Deliverable to those who need them) point-of-care (POC) testing [23].

Measuring glucose concentration is one of the most frequently performed procedures in hospitals and laboratories, since glucose metabolism is crucial to life [24]. Diabetes mellitus and hypoglycemia are clinical conditions associated with abnormal glucose metabolism, with the International Diabetes Federation estimating that 578 million people worldwide will have diabetes by 2030, reaching 700 million by 2045 [25].

Lactate is an intermediate in anaerobic carbohydrate metabolism. At high concentrations in blood, it decreases the pH and can cause muscle damage [26]. Lactate concentrations above 5 × 10−3 mol L−1 and pH below 7.25 are indicative of significant lactic acidosis [24]. Lactate measurement provides a less invasive way to control diabetes in patients and to diagnose type I glycogen storage disease, which decreases the ability of the liver to produce glucose-6-phosphate from glucose [27].

The commonest matrix used to determine glucose and lactate is blood, especially for glucose, given the widespread availability of commercial glucometers. However, this analysis requires an invasive and painful blood collection using needle or finger pricking, which can be stressful if performed several times daily. Saliva is an alternative and very attractive biological matrix for performing clinical analysis [28]. Different to blood sample collection, saliva sample collection is straightforward and can be performed non-invasively and without causing stress, allowing repeated sample collections within short time periods, performed by modestly trained operators [28,29]. Consequently, increasing numbers of studies concerning saliva diagnostics have been published for analytes including glucose [4,30], lactate [26], nitrite [4,31], tyrosine [32], and hormones [33].

The main component of saliva is water (99.5%), with 0.3% of proteins and 0.2% of inorganic compounds and trace substances [34]. Other components found in saliva are shaded squamous epithelial cells, white and red blood cells, microorganisms, and high molecular weight mucins [31,35]. The presence of proteins, large glycoproteins, and cells significantly influences the viscosity of whole saliva [31,34], hampering its analysis using paper devices, especially when multilayer devices (3D-μPADs) are employed. It is possible to minimize the effect of viscosity by precipitating proteins with chemicals, ultrafiltration, or pH adjustment, but these protocols can influence the compounds of interest or the composition of saliva [34], while requiring the use of sophisticated equipment that may not be available in situ or in remote regions where resources are limited [31]. Hence, easier and simpler protocols should be used in the development of low-cost paper devices that can be used by a modestly trained operator.

In this work, a new, simple, and low-cost methodology was developed using a 3D paper-based analytical device for the determination of glucose and lactose in saliva and serum samples, with fluorescence detection. The method employs a simple, fast, and low-cost sample treatment using a cotton-paper-syringe filtration system, enabling 3D-μPAD analysis of saliva samples. The combination of enzymatic reaction with fluorescence detection using carbon dots, produced by a one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis, resulted in a specific and sensitive methodology with low limits of detection. The methodology described here is a valuable analytical tool for the monitoring of glucose and lactate levels.

Glucose (GLU), glucose oxidase (GOx, 200 U mg−1, from Aspergillus niger), lactate (LAC), lactate oxidase (LOx, 40 U mg−1, from Aerococcus viridans), horseradish peroxidase (HRP, 256 U mg−1), tyramine (TYR), citric acid monohydrate (CA), lactose, fructose, ascorbic acid, and uric acid were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrogen peroxide was supplied by Êxodo Científica. Sodium pyruvate, potassium hydrogen phosphate, and potassium dihydrogen phosphate were obtained from Merck. Sucrose was from Mallinckrodt. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) obtained from a Milli-Q system (Millipore) was used to prepare solutions and for dilutions.

A cotton ball (approximately 0.4 g) was introduced into the patient’s mouth to collect the saliva sample (Fig. 1 a). After absorption of the saliva, the cotton was transferred to a syringe fitted with 4 disks (10 mm diameter) of Whatman No. 1 filter paper (Fig. 1b). The piston was introduced and pressure was applied for filtration of the sample using the cotton-paper-syringe system (Fig. 1c). For glucose analysis, the filtered saliva was added directly to the 3D-μPAD, while lactate analysis was performed after 50-fold dilution of the sample in deionized water.

Certified samples of serum were supplied by the Brazilian National Program of Quality Control (PNCQ), which is sponsored by the Brazilian Clinical Analyses Society (SBAC). The certified samples contained glucose and lactate at two different known concentrations, representing normal (level I) and high (level II) levels in the serum of adults. The serum samples were prepared as recommended on the label, with the analysis performed after 200-fold (level I) and 400-fold (level II) dilutions in deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dot synthesis and reagent preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots were synthesized according to the method described by Rossini and co-authors [36]. Citric acid monohydrate and tyramine were used as precursors to generate the fluorescent nanoparticles, employing a simple one-pot microwave synthesis. For the removal of large particles, the synthesized CDs dispersion was centrifuged at 9,000 rpm for 15 min (Universal 320R centrifuge, Hettich), followed by filtration of the supernatant through a 0.45 μm membrane (Millipore). The stock dispersion of CDs was maintained at 4 °C until used.

To prepare the fluorescence reagent employed in the glucose analysis, 4 mL of stock CDs dispersion was added to a 10 mL volumetric flask, with the volume completed using 100 × 10−3 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 6. For lactate analysis, 100 × 10−3 mol L−1 PBS solution was used at pH 6.5. For glucose analysis, a mixture of GOx (120 U mL−1) and HRP (30 U mL−1) was added to a 10 mL volumetric flask, with the volume completed using 100 × 10−3 mol L−1 PBS at pH 6. For lactate analysis, LOx (50 U mL−1) was used instead of GOx, prepared in 100 × 10−3 mol L−1 PBS solution at pH 6.5.

The design employed for the 3D-μPAD (Fig. 2 a) was developed by Morbioli and co-authors [37]. The 3D-μPADs were printed on Whatman No. 1 filter paper using wax toner (Genuine Xerox Solid Black Ink) and a wax printer (Colorqube 8580 DN, Xerox) (Fig. 2b). After printing, the patterns were submitted to thermal treatment for 120 s at 120 °C, in order to melt the wax and form the hydrophobic barriers (Fig. 2c). Each 3D-μPAD was separated from the others. A cut pipette tip was glued into the center of the first layer, for use as a sample holder (Fig. 2d). A 1.0 µL aliquot of the fluorescence reagent dispersion was added to each spot, on the last layer of the 3D-μPAD. After complete drying of the spots (~30 min), 1.0 µL aliquots of the enzyme mixture (GOx + HRP for glucose analysis, or LOx + HRP for lactate analysis) were added to the two center columns and 1.0 µL aliquots of HRP were added to the right and left columns (Fig. 2e). After complete drying of the spots (~30 min), the 3D paper device was folded along the white lines, aligning the edges of adjacent layers and superimposing one layer onto another. The 3D-μPAD was positioned between a bottom flat metal plate, perforated 16 times in a 4 × 4 configuration, according to the bottom layer of the 3D paper device, and two flat magnetic stainless steel sheets on the top (Fig. 2f) [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of biological samples using the 3D-μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: To perform the analysis, a 45 µL aliquot of the saliva or serum sample was added to the pipette tip (sample holder) glued to the top of 3D-μPAD (Fig. 2g). After 10 min, the device was removed from the magnetic apparatus and the bottom layer was separated from the device, for subsequent analysis. After the spots were completely dry (~30 min), the device was positioned in a UV chamber (365 nm irradiation source) and a picture of the bottom layer of the 3D-μPAD was obtained using a camera (Model SX170 IS, Canon) with the following configuration: ISO 200, aperture of f/4.5, and shutter speed of 1/8 s (Fig. 2h). The digital images were analyzed using ImageJ software to obtain the intensity of the blue component (RGB format). The fluorescence quenching was calculated as IS/IB, where IS and IB are the blue component intensities of the sample and the blank, respectively (Fig. 2i), as described previously [36].

The aim of this work was to develop a simple and sensitive method for quantification of glucose and lactate in saliva samples. The method employed a simple filtration using a cotton-paper-syringe system to decrease the viscosity of the saliva sample and enable its analysis using a paper device. The results showed that limits of detection lower than others reported for paper platforms could be achieved using a reagent composed of carbon dots and specific enzymes, coupled with detection employing digital images.

A previous study conducted by our group introduced a novel carbon dot sensitive to hydrogen peroxide in the presence of peroxidase. The CD was synthesized according to an optimized one-pot and one-step microwave-assisted route using citric acid and tyramine as precursors [36]. After the synthesis, the CD dispersion was pale yellow, presented a blue light emission under UV irradiation (365 nm), and exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at 440 nm when excited at 355 nm.

The citric acid was the main carbon source and was essential for polymerization, formation of nanoparticles, and functionalization with tyramine. The tyramine provided nitrogen atoms to the reaction, different from the carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen atoms present in citric acid. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots frequently show increased light emission and quantum yield, compared to non-doped CDs [38–41], as observed for CDs synthesized using only citric acid, which presented low fluorescence and insensitivity to H2O2. The use of precursors with sulfur, phosphorus, boron, or nitrogen atoms in their structures introduces dopants into the CDs, consequently modifying the properties of the material. Nitrogen is an attractive element for doping carbon-based materials, due to its similar atomic size as carbon and its five valence electrons available to promote surface defects [38,42]. The tyramine present on the surfaces of the CDs was responsible for the response to H2O2 by crosslinking of the phenolic residues of tyramine, leading to fluorescence quenching in the presence of HRP.

The glucose and lactate analyses were based on determination of the hydrogen peroxide produced in the presence of glucose oxidase (for glucose) or lactate oxidase (for lactate). In bulk solution, these reactions require sufficient O2 to oxidize the analyte, so the availability of O2 for the enzymatic reaction can be a limiting factor. Paper platforms have a large surface area and are open to the air, so the O2 requirement of the enzymatic reaction is not a limiting factor and the analysis time can be reduced [36].

The H2O2 produced during analyte enzymatic oxidation was determined by fluorescence. In the absence of H2O2, the CDs dispersion exhibited a blue emission under UV light, while the intensity decreased in the presence of H2O2 and HRP, enabling the quantification of glucose and lactate by the quenching of fluorescence, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence quenching was promoted by the hydroxyl groups formed during the reduction of H2O2 catalyzed by the HRP, due to crosslinking of the phenolic groups of tyramine residues present on the CD surface [43–45].

A picture of the μPAD was obtained using a camera in a dark chamber fitted with a UV irradiation source (365 nm). The detection was performed by digital image analysis using the highest sensitivity color channel, which was found to be the blue channel (B), as expected since the CDs used in this work presented blue light fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the figures of merit
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was evaluated considering the following analytical parameters: linear dynamic range, regression equation, precision, repeatability, and limit of detection (LOD). Analytical curves were constructed for glucose and lactate, with fluorescence quenching (I/I0) as a function of the analyte concentration (Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM) Fig. S1). The LOD was calculated according to the expression [46]: LOD = 3SDBlank/B, where SDBlank is the standard deviation of the blank signal (ten replicates) and B is the slope of the linear dynamic range.

The linear ranges, regression equations, coefficients of variation, and limits of detection are provided in Table 1 . The coefficients of variation with R ≤ −0.99 indicated excellent agreement between the data and the regression model for the analytical curve.

The repeatability study evaluated the precision of the proposed method, using independent tests employing the same method, under identical conditions, in the same laboratory, with the same operator and the same equipment [47]. The repeatability assays were performed by repetition of two different concentrations from the analytical curve, at different times on the same day (intra-day) and on different days (inter-day). For each repetition, eight spots of a 3D-μPAD were used. The repeatability was expressed using the relative standard deviations (%RSD) of the intra-day and inter-day repetitions. The concentrations used were 10.0 × 10−6 and 40.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 for glucose and 5.00 × 10−6 and 15.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 for lactate. The %RSD values obtained for the lowest and the highest concentration of glucose were 3.82% and 5.78% (intra-day) and 5.08% and 6.61% (inter-day), while the corresponding values for lactate were 5.43% and 4.84% (intra-day) and 5.88% and 5.85% (inter-day). These results could be considered satisfactory [48], demonstrating that the proposed methods were repeatable and that the 3D-μPAD with fluorescence detection could be used for the determination of glucose and lactate.

For glucose analysis, evaluation was made of the possible interferences of lactate, uric acid, sucrose, lactose, and fructose. For lactate analysis, the potential interferents considered were ascorbic acid, glucose, uric acid, and pyruvate. In the presence of glucose oxidase and peroxidase, only glucose caused significant fluorescence quenching of the CDs (Fig. S2a, ESM). In the presence of lactate oxidase and peroxidase, only lactate quenched the fluorescence of the CDs (Fig. S2b, ESM). The other compounds presented fluorescence quenching values approximately equal to the blank, showing no significant interference in the analysis of either glucose or lactate. As expected, the results obtained in the interference study showed that the enzymatic systems were specific for determination of the analytes.

The accuracy of the proposed methodology was evaluated using standard addition and recovery experiments. For the standard additions, instead of direct mouth sampling using a cotton ball, the saliva sample was collected in a vial. Aliquots of 1 mL of saliva were then transferred to other vials, with addition of glucose at concentrations of 10.0 × 10−6, 20.0 × 10–6, and 30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1, and lactate at 5.0 × 10–6, 10.0 × 10−6, and 15.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. A cotton ball was introduced in each vial to absorb the entire sample volume, followed by transfer to a syringe, according to the procedure described in Section 2.2, and analysis as described in Section 2.5.

The recoveries obtained were 93, 108, and 104% for additions of 10.0 × 10−6, 20.0 × 10–6, and 30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 of glucose, respectively, and 89, 92, and 94% for additions of 5.0 × 10–6, 10.0 × 10−6, and 15.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 of lactate, respectively. The results indicated that the proposed methodology was accurate and that the sample matrix had no significant influence on the glucose and lactate determinations [48].

The proposed method was validated using certified materials for clinical applications provided by PNCQ. The certified samples were prepared and diluted as described in Section 2.2. Analysis was made of two different serum samples containing lactate and glucose at two different levels (Serum I and Serum II).

The concentrations of glucose and lactate found for the serum samples are provided in Table 2 . The errors obtained for these determinations were lower than 7%, showing the satisfactory precision of the proposed method. The concentrations obtained using the method described in this work were compared with the certified concentrations using the t-test. The calculated t-values did not exceed the critical value (4.303), showing that there were no significant differences between the concentrations, confirming the accuracy of the proposed method.

Four saliva samples were collected and prepared as described in Section 2.2. The concentrations of glucose and lactate present in these samples were determined using the methodology developed in this study. The results (Table 3 ) showed that the concentrations were within the normal ranges for glucose (21–38 × 10−6 mol L−1) [30] and lactate (1.0–7.5 × 10−4 mol L–1) [26,49] in saliva.

Tables S1 and S2 (ESM) show the LOD values and detection methods employed in previous studies reported in the literature, where paper-based analytical devices were used to quantify glucose and lactate. The commonest detection methods were colorimetry [4,50–54] and electrochemical techniques [55–57], while fluorimetry was the least used method [58]. Among the studies considered, the enzymatic reactions used in this work were typically employed in the analysis of glucose and lactate, due to their specificity.

Over the years, researchers have attempted to modify the papers used in these devices, in order to achieve lower LODs. The use of chitosan for immobilizing enzymes and reagents helped to reduce the lack of homogeneity in the detection zone, enabling achievement of LODs in the range of tens of μmol L−1 [50,59]. Other modifications such as the use of MOFs (metal–organic frameworks) [54] and a 3D-μPAD with immobilization of enzymes using chitosan [51] have made the determination even more sensitive, reaching LODs of a few μmol L−1. However, the device using MOFs [54] was constructed with a single channel, which did not allow multiplexed or replicated reactions. In the case of the 3D-μPAD device with chitosan [51], a linear relationship was obtained by using the logarithm of the analyte concentration, permitting a wider linear range, although differentiation between similar concentrations was poor and could lead to incorrect results. The LODs achieved for the analysis of glucose and lactate in the present work were among the lowest, in comparison to the values found in the literature (Tables S1 and S2, ESM), reaching 10−6 mol L−1 and 10−7 mol L−1 levels, respectively.

In the analyses of saliva samples reported previously, the paper device used was composed of only one layer of paper, or was a 2D-μPAD [4,53,60], without the multiple layers of paper employed here. The viscosity of saliva hinders fluid transport through the paper, which is why earlier studies have avoided this issue by adding the saliva directly to spots on the 2D-μPAD [53,60]. Other studies have used artificial saliva samples, without the proteins and other constituents of natural saliva [60], or have used wearable devices exposed to a large volume of sample in the person’s mouth [4]. An additional centrifugal step, prior to the analysis, could be used to reduce the viscosity of the sample, although this would require laboratory equipment, consequently reducing portability [53]. The filtration step proposed here enables the use of 3D-μPADs, without any need for complicated and time-consuming sample preparation stages.

A novel methodology was developed and validated for the quantification of glucose and lactate in saliva samples. The collection and treatment of saliva samples is simple, fast, low cost, and can be performed in situ, without any requirement for sophisticated instrumentation. The cotton-paper-syringe system employed to collect and filter the sample reduces the viscosity of the saliva, allowing the use of paper devices with multiple layers of paper, without the need for centrifugation or other pretreatments. After collection, the filtered sample can be directly analyzed using the paper platform.

A 3D-μPAD was constructed using wax printing to create hydrophobic barriers and a magnetic apparatus to hold the device in the correct position. The fluorimetric detection employed a dispersion of CDs synthesized according to a simple, rapid, one-step, eco-friendly, and one-pot microwave-assisted technique, using inexpensive equipment. The analytical methodology developed in this work offers advantages including high detectability, non-invasive sampling, simplicity, low cost, minimal generation of waste, portability, ease of use, and eco-friendly characteristics.

Eduardo Luiz Rossini: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - original draft. Maria Izabel Milani: Methodology, Validation. Liliane Spazzapam Lima: Methodology. Helena Redigolo Pezza: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support provided by the São Paulo State Research Foundation (FAPESP, grants #2016/20847-6 and #2015/21733-1) and the Brazilian National Research Council (CNPq, grant #140503/2016-1), and to Flávia Pavarina (NAC-UNESP) for providing the certified samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119285.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Patterned paper as a platform for inexpensive, low-volume, portable bioassays

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.W. Martinez, S.T. Phillips, M.J. Butte, G.M. Whitesides, Patterned paper as a platform for inexpensive, low-volume, portable bioassays, Angew. Chemie - Int. Ed. 46 (2007) 1318–1320.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Three-dimensional microfluidic paper-based device for multiplexed colorimetric detection of six metal ions combined with use of a smartphone

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Li, Y. Hu, Z. Li, J. Liu, L. Guo, J. He, Three-dimensional microfluidic paper-based device for multiplexed colorimetric detection of six metal ions combined with use of a smartphone, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 411 (2019) 6497–6508.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pump-free microfluidic rapid mixer combined with a paper-based channel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Jang, D. Carrao, R. Menger, A.R. Moraes de Oliveira, C.S. Henry, Pump-free microfluidic rapid mixer combined with a paper-based channel, ACS Sensors. (2020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Salivary diagnostics on paper microfluidic devices and their use as wearable sensors for glucose monitoring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.F. de Castro, S. V. de Freitas, L.C. Duarte, J.A.C. de Souza, T.R.L.C. Paixão, W.K.T. Coltro, Salivary diagnostics on paper microfluidic devices and their use as wearable sensors for glucose monitoring, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 411 (2019) 4919–4928.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in paper-based microfluidic devices

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Technical aspects and challenges of colorimetric detection with microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) - A review
